text: Should you charge for quotes? This is a question I get asked almost daily, sometimes twice a day, and it's a serious topic.
There's so much emotion tied up around charging for quotes. A lot of tradies are really unsure about how to handle this,
and it's no wonder that there's so much fear about whether charging for quotes will lose you work. So let's clear this up.
Yes, you should be charging for quotes. Now, I can hear some of you already. If I start charging, I won't get any jobs.
But actually the opposite tends to happen. You end up attracting better quality clients than winning more of the right kind of work.
Now, I'm not saying you just blurt out. That'll be $150 for a quote and expect everyone to cheer. You have to build it into your sales process.
You need to educate your potential clients, and that education starts from the very first interaction. Whether they call you, send an inquiry email,
or even wander up to you on site, you're already guiding them through an experience. It's not just about offering a quote.
You are providing professional advice. You're giving them a report, an assessment, expert guidance, not just a random number scribbled on a scrap
of paper like everyone else does for free. Uh, think about it like this. How many quotes have you done
in the last six months? Write that number down. Now multiply it by $50. That's the amount of money you could have put in your pocket
just by charging a small fee for your time, expertise, and advice. And honestly, many tradies are charging even more.
Some charge 150, 300, even $450 for a detailed proposal, and people happily pay it when they see the value.
Why? Because they want expert advice and they trust that you'll deliver a quality job. 99% of the time they've already decided they wanna work with you.
It's essentially a deposit, a commitment. That's the whole point. A proper sales process, including charging for quotes, prepositions your clients to want to work with you
before you even send the official price. It's about creating a mindset shift for you and for them. I get it. It can feel scary at first.
It'll go against everything you've probably done up until now. But once you implement a simple structured sales process, you'll wonder why you didn't do it sooner.
If someone isn't willing to pay for your time to drive out, assess the job, create a detailed proposal, and then follow up, well, they probably weren't going
to be a good client anyway. You're not here to be everything to everyone. You're here to work with the right people.
And charging for quotes is one of the fastest ways to filter out the tire kickers and time wasters. It frees you up to do your best work for the people
who genuinely value what you offer. So if you wanna know how to structure this properly, go watch our sales process training.
We break down exactly how to educate your clients, how to script those conversations, and how to confidently move people through a process that
Leads to better clients, better jobs, and a more profitable business. Even if you decide not to charge for quotes just yet,
simply shifting your mindset to value your time and expertise will change how you qualify people. You'll naturally start screening better,
protecting your time and attracting better clients because you are valuable. Your skills, your years of experience, your advice, it's worth something from the very first interaction,
not just after the job's done. So stop selling yourself short, start respecting your own time and start charging for quotes.
